coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots decorated on the ultrafine metal sulfide nanoparticles implanted hollow layered double hydroxides nanocages as new-type anodes for potassium-ion batteries

Wang, Lin
Zhao, Weifang
Xu, Xinyue
Li, Zhe
Li, Yongxiu
Zhou, Tengfei
Hu, Juncheng
coredata.dc:description: Although layered double hydroxides (LDH) stands out among various electrode materials with considerable theoretical capacities, the intrinsically poor electronic conductivity and inevitable aggregations of the fragile 2D structure impede their practical application in rechargeable metal-ion battery. In the present work, nickel/cobalt LDH hollow nanocages derived from metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) were synthesized. Afterward, the ultrafine metal sulfide nanoparticles were implanted into the LDH nanocages (LDH-S) through a in-situ sulfide method. Such a LDH-S architecture achieves an initial discharge capacity of 1387 mAh g−1 and stable specific capacity of 391 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 after 200 cycles while using as potassium-ion battery anode material. In addition, when LDH-S nanocages are decorated with carbon dots (CDs@LDH-S), it exhibits an excellent long-term lifespan achieving 188 mAh g−1 after 8000 cycles at 1.0 A g−1. With the aid of ex-situ characterization and density functional theory (DFT) calculation, the excellent performance of CDs@LDH-S can be contributed to the hetero-structure, in which the interlayer confinement of ultrafine metal sulfides enhances the electrical/ionic conductivity. Furthermore, the well-defined internal voids and functional shells promise intimate contact between the electrolyte and electrode material, and also could avoid agglomeration during repeated potassium-ion insertion and extraction process. In addition, the presence of carbon dots (CDs) further enhances the electrical conductivity and stabilizes the electrode structure. This work proves that LDH-based anode with high capacity and low cost may be one of the promising candidates for potassium-ion storage and the introduction of CDs maybe an efficient way for construction of high-efficient electrode materials for potassium-ion storage.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots decorated on the ultrafine metal sulfide nanoparticles implanted hollow layered double hydroxides nanocages as new-type anodes for potassium-ion batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots decorated on the ultrafine metal sulfide nanoparticles implanted hollow layered double hydroxides nanocages (CDs@LDH-S) were employed as anode for potassium-ion battery. CDs@LDH-S exhibits an excellent long-term lifespan achieving 188 mAh g−1 after 8000 cycles at 1.0 A g−1.

Although layered double hydroxides (LDH) stands out among various electrode materials with considerable theoretical capacities, the intrinsically poor electronic conductivity and inevitable aggregations of the fragile 2D structure impede their practical application in rechargeable metal-ion battery. In the present work, nickel/cobalt LDH hollow nanocages derived from metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) were synthesized. Afterward, the ultrafine metal sulfide nanoparticles were implanted into the LDH nanocages (LDH-S) through a in-situ sulfide method. Such a LDH-S architecture achieves an initial discharge capacity of 1387 mAh g−1 and stable specific capacity of 391 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 after 200 cycles while using as potassium-ion battery anode material. In addition, when LDH-S nanocages are decorated with carbon dots (CDs@LDH-S), it exhibits an excellent long-term lifespan achieving 188 mAh g−1 after 8000 cycles at 1.0 A g−1. With the aid of ex-situ characterization and density functional theory (DFT) calculation, the excellent performance of CDs@LDH-S can be contributed to the hetero-structure, in which the interlayer confinement of ultrafine metal sulfides enhances the electrical/ionic conductivity. Furthermore, the well-defined internal voids and functional shells promise intimate contact between the electrolyte and electrode material, and also could avoid agglomeration during repeated potassium-ion insertion and extraction process. In addition, the presence of carbon dots (CDs) further enhances the electrical conductivity and stabilizes the electrode structure. This work proves that LDH-based anode with high capacity and low cost may be one of the promising candidates for potassium-ion storage and the introduction of CDs maybe an efficient way for construction of high-efficient electrode materials for potassium-ion storage.

ce:section.ce:para: Considering the advantages including cost-efficiency, earth-abundant as well as relatively low redox potential, potassium-ion batteries (PIBs) captures considerable research interests in recent years. Great efforts have been made to construct suitable electrode materials to accommodate the large ionic radius and high intercalation barrier [1–5]. Nevertheless, the extensive development of PIBs is still struggled by serious volume change and sluggish kinetics of the electrode materials. Therefore, the design of high efficient electrode materials with long-term stability remains a significant challenge.

Layered double hydroxides (LDH) have attracted considerable attention because of their large basal spacing structure, adjustable chemical composition, high redox activity and low cost [6–8]. Their ion intercalation/deintercalation ability along with the change in the valence state of the metal ions in the host layers goes well with the basic mechanism of rechargeable alkali metal-ion batteries [9,10]. Furthermore, the channels within the layers of LDHs as well as the channels located between the layers supply plenty of diffusion pathways for lithium/sodium/potassium ions. Therefore, in theory, LDHs could be regarded as interesting electrode materials for alkali metal-ion batteries [11–13]. However, their intrinsically low electronic conductivity severely hampers electron/ion transfer kinetics and inevitable aggregations of the fragile 2D structure impede their practical application in rechargeable metal-ion battery [14,15].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with long-range 3-dimensional order crystalline arrays and uniform micro-pores have been utilized as outstanding templates to synthesis LDH with controllable architecture [16–18]. In this instance, fabricating highly dispersed LDH sheets with a 3D network can protect the fragile LDH structures [19,20]. In addition, the well-defined internal voids and functional shells promise intimate contact between the electrolyte and the electrode active material, and also release the mechanical pressure to accommodate the volume expansion as well, thereby suppressing the deterioration of the material structure and exhibiting superior long-term cycling stability [21].

To enhance electronic conductivity of LDH, a second material such as conductive carbon matrix, MXenes, and transition metal compound have been introduced to modify the structure and properties of LDH-based materials [22–26]. The LDH/metal sulfides heterostructures which could be obtained by in situ sulfurization are of great potentials due to its high conductivity and convenient synthesis process [27,28]. The uniform distribution of highly conductive metal sulfides domains construct a conductive network to achieve high-speed electron and ion transport. Meanwhile, the LDH shell could prevent the aggregation of metal sulfides domains during the ultralong cycle span. For example, NiCo-LDH/Co9S8 was fabricated by using MOFs as precursor and the hybrid system displays superior electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance and high-efficient supercapacitive energy storage ability [15]. Also, cobalt sulfide quantum dots were embedded into the LDH nanosheets and this composite could be used for high-performance hybrid supercapacitors [29]. The LDH/Co9S8 heterostructure was also used as an efficient cathode for lithium-sulfur battery [14]. To date, however, less is known for LDH/metal sulfides complex as favorable electrode materials for alkali metal ion battery, especially for the potassium-ion battery (PIB).

Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as attractive carbonaceous materials for Li+/Na+/K+ battery because carbon dots not only improve electrical conductivity, but also create an excellent interface between electrodes and electrolytes, thereby improving charge transfer resistance [30–32]. For example, Lu et al. introduced carbon dots on the reduced graphene oxide surface as high performance PIBs anode. The introduction of CDs with oxygen-containing functional groups provide a good dispersity in the electrolyte and form a stable SEI on the electrode surface, thereby maintaining the integrity of the electrode structure [30]. Hou et al. reported the carbon quantum dot micelles tailored hollow carbon anode for fast potassium and sodium storage [32]. Furthermore, LDH-carbon dots hybrid show excellent electrocatalytic water oxidation performance [33], however, LDH-carbon dots based nanocomposites as an efficient anode material for PIBs have never previously been reported, to the best of our knowledge.

Inspired by the above considerations, herein, the hollow NiCo-LDHs nanocages are fabricated with ZIF-67 as precursor at first. Afterwards, in-situ sulfurization leads to the interlayer confinement of ultrafine metal sulfides into LDH (LDH-S). At last, carbon dots (CDs) are decorated on the surface of LDH-S nanocages to form CDs@LDH-S hybrid. The CDs@LDH-S nanocomposites are used an efficient anode material for PIBs. The uniform distribution of highly conductive metal sulfides domains construct a conductive network to achieve high-speed electron and ion transports. Meanwhile, the LDH shell with 3D network could prevent the aggregation during the ultralong cycle span. In addition, the presence of carbon dots (CDs) further enhances the electrical conductivity and stabilizes the electrode structure. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations locate the favorable potassium transfer route between the LDH layers with low energy barriers (<0.36 eV). The binding energy of interlayer potassium is lowered by incorporating Co9S8 cluster which facilitates the ion transfer and electrical conductivity by modifying the geometric and electronic structures. The presented discovery demonstrates that the LDH-based hybrid are promising candidates for potassium-ion storage and the introduction of CDs maybe an efficient way for construction of high-efficient electrode materials for potassium-ion storage.
Synthesis of ZIF-67 polyhedrons: ZIF-67 with uniform polyhedrons are synthesized by using a refined ligand-assisted method according to previous works [34,35].

Synthesis of LDH: Briefly, ZIF-67 (30 mg) nanoparticles were dispersed in ethanol (20 mL) to form solution I. Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (100 mg) was dissolved in ethanol (5 mL) to form solution II. After Solution II adding into solution I, the mixture was ultrasound for 90 min to obtain a green product. The product was washed with ethanol for 4 times and then the as-prepared samples were dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven. To ensure the complete conversion of the ZIF-67 template, the product was refluxed at the temperature of 90 °C for 30 min in a mixture solution (50 mL ethanol and 10 mL deionized water).

Synthesis of LDH-S: Briefly, 10 mg of LDH and 20 mg of thioacetamide were dissolved in methanol (20 mL) separately. Both of the above solutions were mixed rapidly and moved into a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 120 °C for 2 h. Afterward, the generated LDH-S particles were washed with ethanol 2 times (3000 rpm, 5 min) and dried in a vaccum oven. Moreover, the effects of the reaction temperature, sulfurization time and amount of added thioacetamide (TAA) were also investigated. The results are listed in the Supporting Information.

Synthesis of carbon dots (CDs): Briefly, 1.05 g of citric acid was dissolved in a solution mixture of water (10 mL) and ethylenediamine (0.34 mL) [36]. And then, a hydrothermal reaction was carried out at 200 °C for 5 h. After the mixture were cooled down to room temperature naturally, a centrifuge process was given with a high speed of 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove any large particles. Afterward, the as obtained product was dried at 80 °C in a vacuum oven.

Synthesis of CDs@LDH-S: Briefly, 10 mg of LDH-S and 10 mg of CDs were separately dispersed into ethanol (10 mL). The obtained mixture was stirred for 1 h. After that, the mixture was moved to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 140 °C for 14 h. Finally, the product was centrifuged for collection. Afterward, the product was dried under vacuum at 80 °C for 24 h.
More detailed synthesis method, detailed characterizations technique, detailed electrochemical characterization, detailed DFT calculations were given in the Supporting Information.

The fabrication process of CDs@LDH-S hybrid is illustrated in Scheme 1 . ZIF-67, which is comprised of Co2+ and 2-methylimidazole organic linkers, is served as the sacrificial template [37]. Two processes happens simultaneously when the ZIF-67 precursors are converted into NiCo-LDH: etching of the nanoparticles and growth of LDH nanosheets on the surfaces of the nanocages. According to previous works, the hydrolysis reaction of Ni2+ and ethanol happens first and releases protons (H+). Then, the protons destroy the coordinate bonding between metal ion (Co2+) and organic linkers (2-methylimidazole). Next, the released Co2+ and Ni2+ precipitate orderly hydroxide layers that cover the surface of the precursor by the coprecipitation reaction. With the gradual dissolution of the precursor and the continually growing LDH, hollow NiCo-LDH nanocages are formed [38]. Finally, Sulphur sources (TAA) are added to fabricate the LDH-S heterostructure through hydrothermal reaction. The effects of the reaction temperature, sulfurization time and amount of added TAA were also investigated, and the results are listed in the Supporting Information (Fig. S1 and Fig. S2). According to the solubility product constants of Co and Ni sulfides, the solubility product (Ksp) of cobalt sulfide is much lower than that of nickel sulfide, therefore, the selective synthesis of cobalt sulfide in LDH layers is feasible (Fig. S4). At last, carbon dots are anchored on the surface of LDH-S nanocages via hydrothermal reaction to obtain CDs@LDH-S hybrid.

The morphology evolution process of LDH-S is characterized in details by corresponding Electron Microscope technique (SEM and TEM images). As displayed in Fig. 1 a, the as-obtained ZIF-67 precursor displays a rhombic dodecahedron with uniform particle size of 400–500 nm. Fig. 1(b, c) displays the SEM images of LDH samples, which consist of orderly interconnected nanosheets while keeps the typical morphology of ZIF-67 template. The broken nanocage highlighted by the red circle clearly demonstrates the hollow nature (Fig. 1c). The hollow functional micro/nanostructures ensures the short diffusion distance of the electrolyte and potassium-ions. In addition, the interconnected LDH nanosheets gives a structural integrity with 3D network which facilitate mass transfer. After sulfurization, the dodecahedron morphology is well maintained, and the broken nanocage highlighted by the red circle clearly demonstrates the hollow nature (Fig. 1(d, e)). Fig. 1(f) exhibits the SEM image of CDs@LDH-S, which show typical hollow interior composed of orderly sheets. The addition of carbon dots does not change the morphology feature of LDH-S nanocages.

As shown in Fig. 1(g, h), TEM images also obviously illustrate that LDH-S nanosheets are distributed on the hollow interior. As displayed in Fig. 1i, EDS elemental color mapping has been used to confirm the uniformly distribution of Co, Ni and S throughout the entire hollow nanocage. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1j) shows lattice d-spacings of 0.25 nm, which match well with the (012) plane of NiCo-LDH [39]. The clear interlayer spacings of 0.222 nm and 0.204 nm correspond to the (422) and (420) planes of Co9S8, respectively [40]. The uniform distribution of highly conductive ultrafine Co9S8 domains forms a conductive network to achieve high-speed electron and ion transport [14]. Fig. 1(k) shows that the interlayer distance of the LDHs is 0.84 nm, as observed from the HRTEM images. This structure, with its large basal spacing, is suitable for potassium-ion storage.

The detailed characterizations for CDs and CDs@LDH-S are displayed in Fig. 2 . TEM image (Fig. 2(a)) shows the average size of the typical dots-like CDs is in the range of 5.0–8.0 nm, which agrees well with the defined size of 10 nm for carbon quantum dots [41]. The inset is the orderly and clear lattice fringes with d-spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the plane of (100) of graphite which also indicates the successfully preparation of carbon quantum dots [42]. Fig. 2(b) displays the TEM image of the CDs@LDH-S composite. A large number of carbon dots are anchored on the surface of LDH-S nanocages. The HRTEM image displays a lattice fringe of 0.21 nm in CDs@LDH-S composite, which is assigned to the graphite (100) plane. It is the strong evidence of the formation of CDs@LDH-S nanostructures. The carbon dots are dispersed uniformly in the LDH-S shells, as confirmed by EDX mapping analysis (Fig. 2d). The EDX analysis of CDs@LDH-S proves that the atomic ratios of C, Ni, Co, S and O are 58.7, 5.6, 1.9, 4.8, and 29.0, respectively.

Fig. 2(e) displays the XRD pattern of as-obtained CDs sample. The broad diffraction peak at around 24.6°is indexed to (002) crystal planes of graphitic structure, which is typical characteristic peak of amorphous carbon materials [43]. Fig. 2(f) displays the Raman spectrum of CDs, LDH-S and CDs@LDH-S. Both CDs and CDs@LDH-S display two distinct bands at 1347 cm−1 (D-bands, disorder-induced mode) and 1587 cm−1 (G-bands, graphite band). Fig. 2(g) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs, LDH-S and CDs@LDH-S samples [44]. CDs exhibits two typical absorption peak located at about 200–250 nm and 300–400 nm, which are ascribed to the electron transition from π to π* taking place in the aromatic sp2 carbon of CDs [45]. The two peaks also appears in CDs@LDH-S composite which further proves the formation of hybrid.

Fig. 3 a displays the XRD patterns of the as-prepared LDH and LDH-S samples. The diffraction peaks at 11.34°, 33.5° and 59.98° can be indexed to the (003), (012) and (110) plane reflections of the LDH phase, respectively [46,47]. Notably, the characteristic peaks of ZIF-67 completely disappear, which indicates the successful construction of the NiCo-LDH shell and the complete etching of ZIF-67 [48]. After sulfurization, the (003), (012), and (110) diffraction peaks of LDH are maintained, and the peaks at 29.6° and 31.3° can be assigned to the Co9S8 phase. Notably, the weakened and negatively shifted (003) peak implies that the metal sulfides are embedded in the LDH nanosheets.

For comparison, the effects of the hydrothermal reaction temperature, sulfurization time and amount of added TAA on the sulfurization degree of the heterostructure were also investigated, and the results are shown in Figs. S1 and S2. The Co9S8 phase is evident until the hydrothermal reaction temperature is increased to 120 °C. When the sulfurization time is increased to 4 h, the LDHs are oversulfurized to the Co3S4 phase. When the mass ratio of LDH/TAA is increased to 1:3, the LDHs are oversulfurized to the NiS and Co3S4 phases. The oversulfurized samples show hollow polyhedral structures that consist of numerous nanoparticles, while the nanosheets on the surface become much less prevalent (Fig. S2).

The complete transformation from the ZIF-67 precursor to LDHs was also verified by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 3b). ZIF-67 exhibits typical bands at 1381–1507 (entire ring stretching), 1578 (C-N aromatic stretching), 1305 (C–H vibration), and 425 cm−1 (Co-N stretching) [49]. When converted to LDH, no characteristic bands of ZIF-67 can be found, and the strong absorption peaks at 3500 and 632 cm−1 can be assigned to the –OH groups and H2O molecules in the interlayers of the LDH [15]. The absorption peaks of Co-S stretching at 1096 cm−1 indicate the existence of cobalt sulfide in S-LDH after sulfidation [15]. Fig. S3 shows that ZIF-67 can not completely transform to NiCo-LDH without a reflux process because the typical bands at 1381–1507 cm−1 of ZIF-67 are still observed in the FT-IR spectrum.

Fig. 3c illustrates the pore profile of LDH and LDH-S with the help of N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms. The LDH and LDH-S have high specific surface areas of 57.7 m2/g and 58.4 m2 g−1, respectively, corresponding to the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. Both LDH and LDH-S display typical combined type I/IV isotherms, implying the existence of meso-pores and micro-pores. BJH pore size distribution plots (insert) also proves the pore size distribution is dominated by mesopores and micropores. The high BET surface area and porous feature of LDH based composite facilitate the contact between the electrode materials and electrolyte, which further shortens the potassium-ion diffusion length.

To further analyzed the chemical composition and bonding states of the LDH and LDH-S nanocages, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed. The XPS survey in Fig. S6 proved the S element was introduced into LDH nanocages through hydrothermal sulfurization treatment. For LDH nanocages, regarding the Co 2p spectrum (Fig. 3d), the peaks located at 797.8 and 781.2 eV could be attributed to Co2+, and the peaks appeared at 795.6 and 780.2 eV could be ascribed to Co3+ [18,50]. The Co2+ and Co3+ species coexist in LDH electrode materials. After sulfurization treatment, as for LDH-S, the peaks at 795.1 and 780.0 could be ascribed to conversion of Co2+ into Co3+ by the sulfidation reaction [51]. For the Ni 2p spectra of LDH, the peaks centered at 872.8 and 855.2 eV correspond to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 of Ni2+ species, and the peaks at 856.7 and 874.9 eV could be ascribed to Ni3+. After sulfurization treatment, as for LDH-S, the binding energies centered at 855.2 and 874.3 eV are ascribed to Ni3+, and the peaks at 853.2 and 871.9 eV are attributed to Ni2+. These results indicate the coexistence of Ni2+ and Ni3+ in LDH-S electrodes [52]. The above results prove the coexistence of Co2+/Co3+ and Ni2+/Ni3+ in LDH-S electrodes. The adjustable valence state of the metal ions in the host layers goes well with the basic mechanism of rechargeable alkali metal-ion batteries and thereby leading to superior electrochemical performance. For the S 2p spectrum (Fig. 3f), the peaks at 162.9 and 161.8 eV correspond to the Co-S bond and the peak centered at 168.6 eV could be ascribed to the satellite peak [53].

The electrochemical behavior of CR2032-type coin cells with LDH, LDH-S and CDs@LDH-S as potassium-ion battery anodes were examined, as displayed in Fig. 4 . LDH delivers initial discharge/charge capacities of 1211 mAh g−1 and 533 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1, respectively, giving an initial coulombic efficiency of 44% (Fig. 4a). The specific capacity drops to 186 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles, indicating a rapid loss of almost 65%. As a comparison, LDH-S shows initial discharge/charge capacities of 1387 mAh g−1 and 582 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1, respectively, and the initial coulombic efficiency is 42%. The specific capacity is 391 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles, indicating a relatively slow capacity loss of 33%. CDs@LDH-S delivers initial discharge/charge capacities of 1237 mAh g−1 and 346 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1, respectively. The specific capacity drops to 246 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles, indicating a relatively smooth loss of about 28%. The decoration of CDs on the surface of LDH-S nanocages could further stabilize the electrode structure, thereby reaching high cycling stability.

As depicted in Fig. 4b, the LDH-S electrode delivers high average reversible capacities of 523, 438, 382, 274, 218 and 164 mAh g−1 at various increasing current density. When returned to the initial 0.1 A g−1, an average reversible capacity of 462 mAh g−1 is measured, corresponding to a capacity retention of 88%. Regarding LDH electrode, the average specific capacities are 321, 258, 187, 156, 108 and 74 mA h g−1 at different current densities, respectively. When returned to the initial 0.1 A g−1, an average reversible capacity of 224 mAh g−1 is measured, indicating a lower capacity retention of about 70%. The CDs@LDH-S electrode delivers high average reversible capacities of 305, 272, 236, 203, 187 and 172 mAh g−1 at various increasing current density from 0.1 A g−1 to 5.0 A g−1. When returned to the initial 0.1 A g−1, an average reversible capacity of 293 mAh g−1 is measured, corresponding to a capacity retention of ∼ 96%. The outperforming rate performance of CDs@LDH-S electrode could be ascribed to carbon dots not only improve electrical conductivity, but also create an excellent interface between electrodes and electrolytes, thereby improving charge transfer resistance.

Fig. 4c shows the initial charge–discharge curves of the LDH-S electrode at 0.1 A g−1. It delivers initial discharge/charge capacities of 1387 mAh g−1 and 582 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1, respectively, corresponding to a low initial coulombic efficiency (CE) of 42%. The relatively low initial CE could be attributed to the irreversible trapping of K+ in electrode materials and formation of an solid electrolyte interface film (SEI). The coulombic efficiency of LDH-S electrode gradually increased to > 98% from the third cycle. Furthermore, the obvious long plateaus agree well with the electrochemical behavior from the CV curves. The profile of 60th overlaps with 160th cycle, proving the excellent electrochemical reversibility [54]. Fig. 4d shows the initial charge–discharge curves of the CDs@LDH-S electrode at 0.1 A g−1. It delivers initial discharge/charge capacities of 1237 mAh g−1 and 346 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1, respectively, corresponding to a low initial coulombic efficiency (CE) of ∼30%. Furthermore, the obvious long plateaus agree well with the electrochemical behavior from the CV curves. The profile of 10th overlaps with 100th cycle, also proving the excellent electrochemical reversibility.

Fig. 4e displays CV curves of LDH-S electrodes for PIBs. In the first cycle, an irreversible cathodic peak appears at approximately 0.570 V but disappears in following cycles which can be attributed to the reduction of electrolytes and formation a solid electrolyte interphase (SEI). In addition, a strong reduction peak appears at 0.68 V for the first cathodic scan while a peak at 1.68 V can be observed in the first anodic scan. The two peaks centered at 0.68 V and 1.68 V appears repeatedly in the subsequent cycles, corresponding to the K+ insertion/deinsertion reactions in LDH-S electrode materials [55–57]. For CDs@LDH-S electrodes, in the first cycle, an irreversible cathodic peak appears at approximately 0.49 V can be attributed to the reduction of electrolytes and formation a solid electrolyte interphase (SEI). The two peaks centered at 0.68 V and 1.62 V appears repeatedly in the subsequent cycles, corresponding to the K+ insertion/deinsertion reactions in CDs@LDH-S electrode materials.

Fig. 4g shows the cycling performance of LDH, LDH-S and CDs@LDH-S at 0.5 A g−1 for 1000 cycles. LDH electrode loss almost 84% capacity after 1000 cycles. LDH-S also presents rapid decay after 1000 cycles. The initial specific capacity of LDH-S is 456 mAh g−1, and the capacity decreases to 143 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles, and then the specific capacity decreases to only 65 mAh g−1 after 1000 cycles. The cycling performance at a large current density is poor for both LDHs and LDH-S electrode. Interestingly, when LDH-S is decorated with carbon dots, CDs@LDH-S delivers a long-term lifespan of 200 mAh g−1 after 1000 cycles at 0.5 A g−1. As shown in Fig. 4h, CDs@LDH-S electrode delivers 188 mAh g−1 after 8000 cycles at 1.0 A g−1. As a comparison, the pure carbon dots achieve 92 mAh g−1 after 600 cycles at 1.0 A g−1. The introduction of CDs provide a good dispersity in the electrolyte and form a stable SEI on the electrode surface, thereby maintaining the integrity of the electrode structure and leading to superior cycling performance. As displayed in Table 1 , CDs@LDH-S is superior to most previously reported CDs based anode and ZIF derived anode in potassium-ion battery and this can be ascribed to its fast diffusion kinetics and structural stability [30,58-63].

Fig. S7 displays the Li-ion storage performance of LDH and LDH-S electrode materials. LDH-S delivers much better cycling stability than LDHs in LIBs. The relatively better cycling stability of LDH-S electrode than that of LDH electrode could be contributed to the specific hetero-structures. When the ultrafine metal sulfide species implanted into the LDH intergalleries, the hetero-interfaces ensure superior charge transport and structure stability.

The K-ion storage kinetics behavior of LDH, LDH-S and CDs@LDH-S were deeply investigated by CV data. The CV experimental data were recorded at different scan rates. According to the concept, the current (i) was comprised of the surface-controlled part and the diffusion-controlled part, and the ratio could be calculated by the following equations [64]:
(1)
i = a
v b
(2)
log ( i )
=
b log ( v )
+ log ( a )
where i means peak current, v refers to the scan rate, while a and b are adjustable parameters. The b value gets closer to 0.5 connote diffusion-controlled process, while b = 1 represents the surface capacitance contribution, respectively [65]. As displayed in Fig. 5 , for the LDH electrode, the acquired b values of the cathodic peak and anodic peak are calculated to be 0.55 and 0.60, respectively, corresponding to the diffusion-controlled behavior. Regarding LDH-S, the b values of the cathodic peak and anodic peak are calculated to be 0.54 and 0.52, respectively, showing diffusion-controlled behavior. For CDs@LDH-S electrode, the b values of the cathodic peak and anodic peak are calculated to be 0.79 and 0.91, respectively, which refers to surface capacitance contribution behavior.

More specifically, the pseudocapacitive contribution ratio at different scan rates can be quantitatively revealed with the following equations [66]:

where k1v refers to the capacitive-controlled contribution and k2v1/2 is associated with diffusion-controlled contribution, respectively [67]. Regarding LDH electrode, the capacitive contribution increases from 26% to 45% as the scan rate rising from 0.2 mV s−1 to 1.0 mV s−1. Concerning LDH-S electrode, the calculated capacitive contribution is 59%, 73%, 82% and 86% at the scan rate of 0.2, 0.4, 0.6 and 0.8 mV s−1, respectively. The pseudo-capacitive contribution of LDH-S electrode is higher than that of LDH electrode. For CDs@LDH-S electrode, the calculated capacitive contribution is 70%, 77%, 78%, 89% and 95% at the scan rate of 0.2, 0.4, 0.6 , 0.8 and 1.0 mV s−1, respectively.

Regarding LDH electrode, the diffusion-controlled contribution is attributed to intercalation-type reactions due to: (1) LDHs have a large basal spacing; and (2) the change in valence state of the metal ions in the LDHs layers during the K-ion intercalation/deintercalation process agrees well with the working mechanism of rechargeable metal-ion battery. For LDH-S electrodes, both diffusion-controlled and capacitive-controlled electrochemical behaviors occur simultaneously. The high pseudo-capacitive behavior belongs to the synergistic effect of unique hetero-structure, where the ultrafine metal sulfides are implanted into LDH nanosheets, thereby leading to high conductivity. Furthermore, the capacitive contribution also could be ascribed to the rough surface and hollow interior [68]. For CDs@LDH-S electrodes, the high proportions of pseudo-capacitance contributions leads to fast kinetics, thus the CDs@LDH-S electrode shows superior rate capacity under high current density.

The effect of metal sulfide nanoparticles and carbon dots on the potassium-ion diffusion coefficients (DK+) in LDH-S and CDs@LDH-S electrodes were further explored via galvanostatic intermittent titration technique. This measurement allows the calculation of potassium-ion diffusion coefficient (DK+) according to Fick’s second law, as exhibited in Eq. (5) [69–71]:

As displayed in Fig. 6 , for LDH electrode, the range of Dk+ value is 0.5 × 10−11 cm2 S−1 to 9.0 × 10−11 cm2 S−1 during discharging and 0.5 × 10−11 cm2 S−1 to 300 × 10−11 cm2 S−1 while charging (Fig. 6(a, b, c, d)). For LDH-S electrode, the Dk+ value ranges from 0.5 × 10−10 cm2 S−1 to 2.4 × 10−10 cm2 S−1 during discharging and 1.0 × 10−10 cm2 S−1 to 3000 × 10−10 cm2 S−1 when charging (Fig. 4(e, f, g, h)). More interestingly, when the LDH-S nanosheets are mixed with carbon dots, the range of Dk+ value for CDs@LDH-S is 1 × 10−8 cm2 S−1 to 10 × 10−8 cm2 S−1 during discharging and 0.1 × 10−7 cm2 S−1 to 5 × 10−7 cm2 S−1 when charging (Fig. 4(i, j, k, l)). The DK+ values of LDH-S is two order of magnitudes higher than that of LDH electrode, indicating more efficient kinetics for the intercalation/deintercalation of potassium-ion. By introducing metal sulfide species into LDH matrix, the electrical conductivity is enhanced, furthermore, the heterostructures not only facilitate interfacial electron transport process but also promote the potassium-ion diffusion process. By mixing LDH-S nanosheets with carbon dots, the DK+ values increases up to two order of magnitudes again. The CDs@LDH-S electrode with interconnected nanosheets and carbon dots achieve much fast potassium migration rate, thereby showing excellent rate performance [72]. Carbon dots not only improve electrical conductivity, but also create an excellent interface between electrodes and electrolytes, thereby improving charge transfer resistance.

To obtain more insight into the mechanism of potassium storage for the LDH-based anode, ex situ XPS, ex situ SEM, ex situ HRTEM, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were performed. The chemical state changes of Co and Ni elements during the different potassium-ion intercalation deintercalation states were recorded by ex situ XPS measurements, as exhibited in Fig. 7 (a, b). During discharge state, some of the Co3+ transform to Co2+ due to the insertion of potassium-ion (K+). After the potassium-ion battery is recharged to 2.3 V, the binding energy of Co element turn back to the original positions which means the reversible change of Co2+ to Co3+ in the LDHs electrode materials. The reversible change of Ni2+/Ni3+ also could be observed during the potassium-ion insertion/extraction process. The EIS results are displayed in Fig. 7(c). The semicircle at high frequency of LDH-S is smaller than that of LDH electrode, indicating a lower charge-transfer resistance and higher potassium ion transfer rate during potassium-ion storage process. Fig. 7(d, e, f) shows SEM images of the LDH, LDH-S, CDs@LDH-S electrodes after 1000 cycles at a current density of 0.1 A g−1. The hollow nanocage morphology of the LDH electrode is seriously damaged. In contrast, the LDH-S electrode maintains the hollow morphology in some degree. The CDs@LDH-S could maintain the the integrity of the electrode structure. The interlayer distance of LDHs in different states was also observed from the HRTEM images, as displayed in Fig. 7(g, h). While discharging to 0.4 V, the d-spacing between the LDH layers shows a notable expansion with the width increased from 0.7 nm to 1.0 nm (Fig. 7g). And in the charge process (Fig. 7h), the width of the d-spacing decreases to 0.68 nm. The ex situ HR-TEM characterizations prove the reversible expansion/contraction of LDH-based electrodes during the repeated intercalation/deintercalation of potassium-ion. Besides, it also verify the well structural reversibility of the LDH-based electrode materials.

The potassium-ion transfer mechanism between the LDH layers, and the confinement effect of LDH/cobalt sulfide on the mobility of potassium and the electronic properties of the heterostructure were further elucidated by carrying out DFT calculations (Fig. 8 ). The most favorable potassium transfer pathway in the LDH layers is determined with a low energy barrier of 0.36 eV (Fig. 8a) from the most stable trigonal prism (TP) site to the less stable trigonal edge (TE) site (Fig. 8c), which is similar to the sodium transfer in NiCo-LDH. The binding energy (BE) of potassium in LDH is 2.42 eV (Fig. 8b), which is lowered as 1.32 eV by a neighboring confinement of cobalt sulfide cluster (Co9S8) indicating the improvement of potassium mobility. Moreover, the LDH/Co9S8 confinement greatly influences the electronic charge transfer between the host LDH and the guests of K (Fig. 8d) and Co9S8 (Fig. 8e). The perturbation between the LDH and cobalt sulfide particle favors the electronic conductivity by shrinking and diminishing the band gap of LDH semiconductor by 0.4 and 1.0 eV for the majority and minority spins, respectively (Fig. S8).

In summary, LDH-carbon dots based nanocomposites were fabricated and introduced as a new-type anode for potassium-ion battery. It shows superior long-term cycling performance, with a capacity of 188 mAh g−1 over 8000 cycles at 1 A g−1, as well as excellent rate capability. These favorable electrochemical performance can be attributed to: (1) the abundant metal sulfide species in LDH matrix leads to formation of hetero-junctions, thereby enhancing the electrical conductivity and facilitating the ion transfer; (2) the well-defined internal voids and functional shells promise intimate contact between the electrode active material and the electrolyte while also release the mechanical pressure, thereby suppressing the deterioration of the material structure; (3) the presence of carbon dots further enhances the electrical conductivity and stabilizes the electrode structure. In addition, DFT calculations reveals a low diffusion barrier for LDH-S electrode. This work proves that LDH-based anode with high capacity and low cost may be one of the promising candidates for potassium-ion storage and the introduction of CDs maybe an efficient way for construction of high-efficient electrode materials for potassium-ion storage.
